questions.question: A RISC-V register file has dual-port read and single-port write. How many address inputs does it need to read two source registers and write one destination register in a single cycle?
options: 3 address inputs: rs1_addr (5b), rs2_addr (5b), rd_addr (5b)
2 address inputs: a shared 5-bit address bus and a direction signal
1 address input: a multiplexed 5-bit bus that cycles through all three
32 address inputs: one per register
questions.explanation: The register file needs three independent 5-bit address inputs: rs1_addr and rs2_addr for the two asynchronous reads, and rd_addr for the synchronous write. These are provided simultaneously by the instruction decoder.

questions.question: In RISC-V, register x0 is hardwired to zero. What happens when software writes a non-zero value to x0?
options: The processor raises an illegal instruction exception
The write is silently discarded; x0 continues to read as 0
x0 stores the value until the next reset
The write succeeds but x0 is read-only for privileged code
questions.explanation: Writes to x0 are discarded in hardware — the register file simply ignores the write when rd_addr is 0. This provides a convenient no-op sink and eliminates the need for special NOP encoding.

questions.question: Why is SRAM used for CPU register files and caches instead of DRAM?
options: SRAM does not need a power supply to retain data
SRAM is denser, fitting more bits per square millimeter
SRAM is faster and does not require periodic refresh cycles
SRAM uses only 1 transistor per bit, making it cheaper
questions.explanation: SRAM uses 6 transistors per bit (fast, no refresh) vs. DRAM's 1 transistor + 1 capacitor (dense, needs refresh). The register file needs sub-nanosecond access with no refresh overhead, making SRAM the correct choice despite its higher cost and lower density.

questions.question: A register file uses asynchronous read. When rs1_addr changes, when does rs1_data become valid?
options: On the next rising clock edge
After a propagation delay through combinational logic, within the same cycle
Two clock cycles later, after the address is latched
Only when the write-enable signal is also asserted
questions.explanation: Asynchronous read means the address-to-data path is purely combinational — the output updates after a gate delay as soon as the address changes, with no clock edge required. This is in contrast to synchronous read, where the address is sampled on a clock edge and data appears on the next edge.

questions.question: Why are registers the fastest storage in a CPU?
options: Because they use magnetic storage
Because they are built from flip-flops directly on the CPU die, closest to the ALU
Because they are the largest storage in the system
Because they only store one bit each
questions.explanation: Registers are implemented as flip-flops directly on the processor die, physically adjacent to the ALU and control unit. They have the shortest wire delays and no cache-miss penalty. A typical CPU has 32-64 general-purpose registers accessible in a single clock cycle.
